summary: I once wrote to Dave Barry, the comedian, and signed my name in a way that made him respond with surprise, 'Really?' People often find my chosen name intimidating, much like how Dave Barry reacted. It was a small but memorable interaction that stuck with me.

My husband was finishing his last two years at Lehigh University in Bethlehem, Pennsylvania, while I worked for the telephone company before becoming pregnant. Lehigh was part of our early life together, marking a time when I was balancing work and family plans. It was in that period, around the early 1960s, that our lives were rooted in Bethlehem.

Bethlehem, Pennsylvania, is where my husband was attending Lehigh University when we were starting our family. I worked locally there as a telephone service representative before having my son. It was a foundational place for us in the early 1960s, shaping our early adult lives.

I was fortunate to win a National Defense Education Act fellowship to pursue my PhD, which I hadn't initially planned. This was during the time when Sputnik had just launched, and the government was eager to fund advanced degrees to catch up with Russia. The Act helped me immensely, even though I never repaid the government for their investment.

Sputnik's launch really shook the United States and led to Congress passing the National Defense Education Act to boost advanced education. It was a wake-up call that Russia was ahead in the space race, and this influenced my academic path as I received a fellowship under this Act. The event marked a significant shift in education and national priorities in the late 1950s and early 1960s.

Russia was ahead of us when Sputnik was launched, which caused a lot of concern in the U.S. and led to increased funding for education through the National Defense Education Act. This geopolitical competition indirectly shaped my academic opportunities in the 1960s. It was a time when the U.S. was scrambling to catch up scientifically and technologically.

During the Vietnam War, many graduate schools expanded their programs because attending graduate school was a way to avoid the draft. This led to a surplus of PhDs in English, making it difficult for me to find a job after I completed my degree in 1973. The war had a direct impact on the job market and my career prospects in the early 1970s.

After earning my PhD, I looked for work within fifty to eighty miles of Allentown, Pennsylvania, but found none. This lack of opportunity pushed me to make a big decision to move to Arkansas in the 1970s. Allentown was where I initially hoped to build my career but ultimately had to leave.

In the 1970s, I bought land in Arkansas, in the Ozarks, to start a women's conference center as part of the back-to-the-land movement. I became involved in local environmental activism, particularly fighting against aerial spraying of phenoxy herbicides. Arkansas became a place of both personal and political growth for me during that decade.

The Ozarks in Arkansas was where I settled in the 1970s, buying land to live more simply and engage in activism. The terrain was rugged with hills and ravines, making some agricultural practices like cattle grazing impractical. It was also a community of like-minded people, including friends with advanced degrees, who gathered for political work and social events.

I became a subscriber to Mother Earth News during my time in Arkansas, learning about sustainable living and milking equipment. The magazine was part of my education as I embraced the back-to-the-land lifestyle in the 1970s. It helped me understand practical aspects of farm life and homesteading.

In Madison County, Arkansas, I worked with colleagues to petition to outlaw phenoxy herbicides that were being sprayed to clear forests for cattle pasture. We researched voter registration records at the courthouse to gather signatures and put the issue on the ballot. Although we did not win, it was a significant community effort in environmental activism during the 1970s.

Tyson Foods was one of the powerful opponents we faced in our fight to outlaw phenoxy herbicides in Madison County, Arkansas. Their interests in poultry farming worked against our environmental efforts in the 1970s. It was a tough battle against big industry during my time living in the Ozarks.

State Farm was another opponent in our campaign to ban phenoxy herbicides in Madison County, Arkansas. Their influence, along with Tyson Foods, made it difficult to succeed in the 1970s environmental ballot effort. It showed me how entrenched corporate interests could be in local politics.

I attended a presentation in Eureka Springs about bioenergetics analysis, which led me to learn about an internship opportunity at the Psychiatric Center in Tulsa. This event was a turning point that helped me transition from Arkansas to a career in psychotherapy starting in 1978. Eureka Springs was a stepping stone in my professional journey.

I was selected for a year-long psychotherapy internship at the Psychiatric Center in Tulsa starting the day after Labor Day in 1978. The program paid us and provided health insurance and free psychotherapy, which was invaluable. This experience marked a new chapter in my career in mental health.

Tulsa became my home when I joined the Psychiatric Center internship in 1978 and later worked as a mental health case manager. I also accessed the rheumatoid arthritis clinic at the University of Oklahoma Health Sciences Center there. Tulsa was central to my medical treatment and professional work in the late 1970s and beyond.

My internship at the Psychiatric Center in Tulsa began the day after Labor Day in 1978. That timing marked the start of a significant year-long training that shaped my career in psychotherapy. Labor Day that year was a turning point for me professionally.

entity: University of Oklahoma Health Sciences Center
summary: I received treatment for rheumatoid arthritis at the University of Oklahoma Health Sciences Center's rheumatoid arthritis clinic in Tulsa. They tried various therapies including gold salts injections before newer immune-suppressing medicines became available in the 1990s. The center was crucial in managing my chronic illness.

entity: There Is a Cure for Arthritis
summary: I tried the advice in the book There Is a Cure for Arthritis during the 1970s when my symptoms were worsening. The book emphasized diet changes, like juice fasts and reducing flour and sugar, which helped me lose weight and feel better. It was an important part of managing my arthritis before modern medicines were available.
